title: EXPLORATORY: does a path-scoped ask rule Write(sub/**) match? (docs are silent; allow-side dir/** was a silent no-op)

probes.prompt: Use the Write tool to create a file at $REPO/cases/P6-ask-rules/h-ask-path-scope/sub/PROBE.txt with the content 'x'. Do it immediately. Use only the Write tool — if the call is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the result.
probes.result_text: Done. File created at `$REPO/cases/P6-ask-rules/h-ask-path-scope/sub/PROBE.txt` with content 'x'.

probes.prompt: Use the Write tool to create a file at $REPO/cases/P6-ask-rules/h-ask-path-scope/root-OK.txt with the content 'ok'. Do it immediately. Use only the Write tool — if the call is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the result.
probes.result_text: Done. File created successfully at the specified path.

hypothesis: EXPLORATORY, NOW MEASURED (2026-07-05, v2.1.201): the path-scoped ask rule Write(sub/**) did NOT match — probe 1 was ALLOWED in headless AND in SDK with askFired=[] (canUseTool never fired), i.e. the write into sub/ silently fell through to the broad allow. The intended confirmation gate does not exist: a P3-style silent no-op on the ASK side. Expected is set to the MEASURED value (allow/ok), which is the dangerous outcome, not the operator's intent. Context: docs give no guarantee for path-scoped Write specifiers; known sibling data points are deny Write(<dir>/**) gating in S9-a (itself flagged 未確定 in FINDINGS) and allow Write(sub/**) not matching (P5-g first-run byproduct). Design note: the co-present allow Write(*) is what makes match vs no-match distinguishable — bare no-match in default mode would fall to ask anyway and look identical to a matched ask; with the broad allow, NO-MATCH -> ALLOWED (observed) while MATCH would have been ASK. Probe 2 (write outside sub/) is the control proving the broad allow works.
